1: H. Guyford Stever was president at the time of the merger (1965–1972) and is credited with finalizing the merger with the Mellon Institute of Research. Carnegie Mellon University
2: Women were first admitted in 1906, when the Margaret Morrison Carnegie School for Women opened its doors as part of Carnegie Technical Schools. Wikipedia Full integration of women into the broader university occurred in 1973, when Margaret Morrison College closed and women joined their male peers in all classrooms and dorms. Carnegie Mellon University
3: Richard Cyert served the longest tenure, from 1972 to 1990 — 18 years. IMDb
4: The original Carnegie Tech campus buildings were designed by architect Henry Hornbostel in the early 1900s. The buildings — including what is now Hamerschlag Hall and Baker Hall — were designed in a Beaux-Arts style with Hornbostel concealing functional elements like smokestacks behind classical architectural forms. Carnegie Mellon University
5: With the end of World War II, the latter half of the 20th century brought unprecedented growth to Carnegie Tech. In 1956, the arrival of the first IBM computer on campus was revolutionary, initiating a university culture where information technology pervaded virtually all areas of study. Carnegie Mellon University
6: The Mellon Institute of Industrial Research was founded in 1913 by Andrew Mellon and Richard B. Mellon and was formerly a part of the University of Pittsburgh, bringing significant research capabilities to the merger. Wikipedia
7: Andrew Carnegie funded the construction of the original Carnegie Technical Schools with an initial donation of $1 million, drawn from the fortune he made building Carnegie Steel Company, which he sold to J.P. Morgan in 1901 for over $300 million. CanvasBusinessModel
8: CMU's oldest campus buildings were designed by Henry Hornbostel in an unconventional Beaux-Arts style that adapted classical architecture to Pittsburgh's steeply sloping terrain, with sloping corridors inside Baker and Doherty halls and a temple-like rotunda concealing a smokestack at Hamerschlag Hall. Carnegie Mellon University
9: The College of Engineering: The Carnegie Institute of Technology (commonly referred to as Carnegie Institute of Technology or CIT). Wikipedia
10: The Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences was renamed after William S. Dietrich II pledged a $265 million gift. It was formed from the programs of the former Margaret Morrison Carnegie College and houses departments in English, History, Modern Languages, and Psychology. Carnegie Mellon University
11: The School of Urban and Public Affairs — now known as the H. John Heinz III College — was created during the phase-out of Margaret Morrison Carnegie College, playing a key role in CMU's expansion into public policy and information systems education. Carnegie Mellon University
12: CMU's SCS offers a standalone undergraduate Bachelor of Science in Computer Science, one of the first and most prestigious stand-alone computer science degrees in the world, separate from any engineering college.
13: The Human-Computer Interaction Institute is housed in Newell-Simon Hall, which opened in 2000, and is a leading research center in the study of how people interact with technology, known for pioneering work in usability, interaction design, and accessibility. Carnegie Mellon University
14: CyLab is the university-wide security and privacy institute at Carnegie Mellon University. It coordinates security and privacy research and education across all university departments, and is consistently ranked the top undergraduate cybersecurity program in the U.S. by U.S. News & World Report. Cmu
15: The Simon Initiative was established under President Subra Suresh to enhance the impact of technology-enhanced learning within the university and around the globe, named in honor of Herbert Simon's contributions to education and cognitive science. Carnegie Mellon University
16: The Gates and Hillman Centers, opened in 2009, include 217,000 square feet of classroom, office, and laboratory space. They are home to the School of Computer Science offices, Computer Science Department, Machine Learning Department, and the Language Technologies Institute. Carnegie Mellon University
17: The complex was funded through major gifts from Bill Gates (Microsoft founder) and Henry Hillman (Pittsburgh industrialist and philanthropist).
18: The Cohon University Center contains an indoor swimming pool, bookstore, student club facilities, gym, and cafeteria. It was dedicated in 1996 and renamed in 2014 in recognition of the eighth president of the university, Jared Cohon. Fandom
19: A large grassy area known as "the Cut" forms the backbone of the campus, with a separate grassy area known as "the Mall" running perpendicular. The Cut was formed by filling in a ravine with soil from a nearby hill that was leveled to build the College of Fine Arts building. Fandom
20: Wean Hall, opened in 1971, once housed the bulk of the School of Computer Science and remains home to some SCS faculty, staff, and students. Carnegie Mellon University
21: Sergey Brin, co-founder of Google, completed his undergraduate studies at University of Maryland, but many of Google's foundational algorithms and early engineers came through CMU. Note: this question is sometimes misattributed — Brin did not attend CMU. The more accurate CMU connection is through faculty and researchers who influenced Google's development, such as Raj Reddy's AI work.
22: Andy Grove (András Gróf), legendary Intel CEO who helped build Intel into a global chipmaker, is associated with CMU. Wikipedia More precisely, Intel was co-founded by Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce, neither of whom attended CMU.
23: CMU has been home to 21 Nobel Prize recipients. In Economics, these include Herbert Simon (1978) for research into decision-making, John Forbes Nash Jr. (1994) for game theory, Merton Miller (1990), Franco Modigliani (1985), Oliver Williamson (2009), Finn Kydland (2004), Edward Prescott (2004), Dale Mortensen (2010), and Lars Peter Hansen (2013). Carnegie Mellon University
24: Rob Marshall, film director nominated for a 2003 Academy Award for Chicago, graduated from CMU's School of Drama in 1982. George A. Romero, the legendary horror filmmaker known for Night of the Living Dead, also attended CMU. Wikipedia
25: Herbert A. Simon, who joined Carnegie Mellon in 1949, pioneered cognitive psychology and cognitive science by studying how humans make decisions and solve problems. Along with Allen Newell, he developed models of human problem-solving and founded the field of artificial intelligence. Computer Society
26: Billy Porter graduated from CMU's drama program in 1991 and went on to earn a Grammy, Tony, and Emmy. Judith Light graduated from CMU's drama program in 1970. Patina Miller won the Tony Award for Best Actress in a Musical for her role in Pippin. Pittsburgh Magazine
27: CMU has been home to 13 Turing Award recipients, including Alan Perlis (1966, first-ever Turing Award winner), Allen Newell and Herbert Simon (jointly, 1975 for AI), Dana Scott (1976), Robert Floyd, Raj Reddy (1994), Manuel Blum (1995), Edmund Clarke (2007), Leslie Valiant, William Mattis (2012), and Geoffrey Hinton (2018). Wikipedia
28: John Forbes Nash Jr., who earned his B.S. and M.S. from Carnegie Mellon (then the Carnegie Institute of Technology) in 1948, won the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 1994 for his pioneering analysis of equilibria in the theory of non-cooperative games. Carnegie Mellon University
29: Raj Reddy is one of the founding figures of the Robotics Institute at CMU, established in 1979. He served as the first director and went on to win the Turing Award in 1994.
30: Vinod Khosla (M.S. 1978) co-founded Sun Microsystems; James Gosling created the Java programming language during his time at CMU; Feng-hsiung Hsu (Ph.D. 1990) co-created the Deep Blue chess computer at IBM. Wikipedia
31: The North Shore is home to several Pittsburgh landmarks including Acrisure Stadium (home of the Steelers), PNC Park (home of the Pirates), and an eleven-foot bronze statue of Fred Rogers along the riverfront. Wikipedia
32: The South Side (South Side Flats and South Side Slopes) is known for East Carson Street, Pittsburgh's main entertainment corridor, lined with bars, restaurants, and nightlife venues, as well as the South Side Slopes with their famous public staircases.
33: Lawrenceville was founded in 1814 by William B. Foster (father of composer Stephen Foster) and is named for Captain James Lawrence, hero of the War of 1812. It has transformed from an industrial center into one of Pittsburgh's trendiest neighborhoods, with Butler Street as its main artery housing restaurants, bars, boutiques, and coffee shops. Wikipedia
34: Members of the Negley Family were early inhabitants of the East Liberty area, establishing themselves there in 1778. Today, East Liberty has undergone major urban revitalization and is known for its tech companies, restaurants, and cultural development, with major employers like Google having established offices there. Libguides
35: The Hill District is a historically African American neighborhood that was known as "the Crossroads of the World" by Harlem Renaissance poet Claude McKay. Beginning in the years leading up to World War I, it was the cultural center of Black life in Pittsburgh and a major center of jazz, home to legendary venues like the Crawford Grill. Wikipedia
36: The Northside is known as the home of the Andy Warhol Museum, the Children's Museum of Pittsburgh, the National Aviary, and the August Wilson African American Cultural Center, as well as Acrisure Stadium and PNC Park on its eastern edge (North Shore).
37: Bloomfield is known for staying true to its roots with authentic cuisine, community festivals, and chain-free shopping. It is often called Pittsburgh's "Little Italy" for its strong Italian-American heritage and community character. Visit Pittsburgh
38: Homewood is a predominantly Black neighborhood with rich arts and cultural history. It is known as the subject of writer John Edgar Wideman's Pittsburgh novels and is undergoing arts-focused community revitalization efforts. Visit Pittsburgh
39: Polish Hill maintains deep Eastern European roots dating to the late 1800s and early 1900s, when many Polish immigrants settled there to work in the steel mills and railroads. The Immaculate Heart of Mary Church stands as the heart of this close-knit community, which still honors its Polish heritage and traditions. Straightouttapittsburgh
40: Pittsburgh played a critical role as an industrial and manufacturing hub supplying Union forces with iron, steel, weapons, and ammunition. The city's Allegheny Arsenal was a major federal munitions depot, and Pittsburgh's iron foundries were essential to the Union war effort.
41: The Homestead Strike was a violent labor dispute between the Carnegie Steel Company and its workers that occurred in 1892 in Homestead, Pennsylvania. On July 6, private security guards hired by the company arrived, exchanged gunfire with workers, and at least three guards and seven workers were killed. It remains one of the most pivotal events in U.S. labor history. Encyclopedia Britannica
42: Pittsburgh's steel industry began its major decline in the 1970s and accelerated sharply in the late 1970s and early 1980s, during the broader "Rust Belt" deindustrialization. By the mid-1980s, most of the major steel mills in the Pittsburgh region had closed, resulting in widespread unemployment and population loss.
43: Andrew Carnegie built Pittsburgh's Carnegie Steel Company, which he sold to J.P. Morgan in 1901 for $303,450,000, making it the U.S. Steel Corporation. After selling, he devoted his life to philanthropy, building Carnegie Hall in New York, founding Carnegie Mellon University, creating the Carnegie Museums of Pittsburgh, and establishing libraries across the country. IMDb
44: The Pittsburgh Renaissance was a major mid-20th century urban renewal effort spearheaded by Mayor David Lawrence and Richard King Mellon that transformed downtown Pittsburgh from a smoke-choked industrial city into a cleaner, modernized urban center, including the development of Gateway Center and Point State Park.
45: David Lawrence served as Mayor of Pittsburgh from 1946 to 1959 and later as Governor of Pennsylvania. He was the driving force behind the Pittsburgh Renaissance, partnering with the business community to dramatically clean up air pollution, redevelop downtown, and begin Pittsburgh's post-industrial transformation.
46: PPG Place is known for its distinctive Gothic Revival-style glass tower design in downtown Pittsburgh. It is a complex of six buildings featuring more than one million square feet of PPG glass, designed by Philip Johnson and John Burgee, with distinctive Gothic spires. The plaza features an ice rink in winter. Tripadvisor
47: Fallingwater is the iconic Frank Lloyd Wright-designed house built over a waterfall for the Kaufmann family in 1939. It is located in Mill Run, Pennsylvania, approximately 70 miles southeast of Pittsburgh, and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most celebrated works of 20th-century architecture.
48: The Clayton mansion is the historic home of industrialist Henry Clay Frick, located in the Point Breeze neighborhood of Pittsburgh. It is preserved as part of The Frick Pittsburgh museum complex, which also includes the Frick Art Museum and Car and Carriage Museum.
49: The Fort Pitt Museum is located in Point State Park at the confluence of Pittsburgh's three rivers. It tells the story of the French and Indian War, the struggle for control of the Forks of the Ohio, and the founding of Pittsburgh as Fort Pitt by the British in 1758.
50: Primanti Bros. is known for its signature sandwiches made of grilled meat, melted cheese, an oil-and-vinegar-based coleslaw, tomato slices, and French fries between two thick slices of Italian bread. Founded in Pittsburgh's Strip District in 1933, it was named one of "America's Classic" restaurants by the James Beard Foundation Awards in 2007. Wikipedia
51: Pierogies are deeply embedded in Pittsburgh's working-class immigrant culture, brought by Eastern European immigrants — particularly Polish, Ukrainian, and Slovak — who settled in Pittsburgh to work in the steel industry. Pittsburgh is famous for its pierogi culture, celebrating them at festivals and sporting events. The Pittsburgh Pirates even have a "Pierogi Race" mascot tradition at home games.
52: Pamela's Diner is a beloved Pittsburgh breakfast institution known for its crepe-style hotcakes (thin, crispy-edged pancakes), hash browns cooked in butter, and unpretentious diner atmosphere. It became nationally known when President Barack Obama visited during a campaign stop and praised the food.
53: The Strip District was founded as a busy industrial center along the Allegheny River and is now home to the original Primanti Bros. location (founded 1933), along with a vibrant food market culture featuring specialty grocers, fishmongers, ethnic bakeries, produce markets, and restaurants. Wikipedia
54: The Pittsburgh area is home to Duquesne University, Chatham University, Robert Morris University, Carlow University, Point Park University, La Roche University, and Allegheny County Community College, among others.
55: Duquesne University is a private Catholic research university on Pittsburgh's bluff, known for its law school, pharmacy school, School of Music (Tamburitzans folk ensemble), and its strong liberal arts tradition rooted in the Spiritan Catholic order.
56: The Pittsburgh Promise is a scholarship program offering financial support to graduates of Pittsburgh Public Schools who attend college or post-secondary institutions in Pennsylvania. It was launched in 2008 and funded in part by UPMC and other major Pittsburgh institutions to encourage students to stay and study in the region.
57: Pittsburgh's public school system is called Pittsburgh Public Schools, serving the City of Pittsburgh with dozens of elementary, middle, and high schools.
58: Major corporations headquartered in Pittsburgh include PNC Financial Services, UPMC, PPG Industries, Heinz (now Kraft Heinz), U.S. Steel, Wesco International, Dick's Sporting Goods, and EQT Corporation, among others.
59: H.J. Heinz Company was founded in Sharpsburg, Pennsylvania (near Pittsburgh) in 1869 by Henry John Heinz. It grew into one of the world's largest food companies, famous for its "57 Varieties" slogan and iconic ketchup. The company merged with Kraft Foods in 2015 to form The Kraft Heinz Company, though it retains deep roots in Pittsburgh.
60: PNC Financial Services is one of the largest banks in the United States, headquartered in Pittsburgh. It was formed through the merger of Pittsburgh National Corporation and Provident National Corporation in 1983. PNC Tower is a landmark of Pittsburgh's downtown skyline.
61: Innovation Works is Pittsburgh's most active early-stage investor and a leading startup accelerator. It provides funding, mentorship, and resources to technology startups in southwestern Pennsylvania, with particular strength in robotics, AI, and advanced manufacturing.
62: The Pittsburgh Riverhounds SC is Pittsburgh's professional soccer team, competing in the USL Championship (second division of American soccer). They play at Highmark Stadium on the South Side Riverfront.
63: The Pittsburgh Crawfords were a perennial championship team in the Negro Major Leagues that played at Greenlee Field in the Hill District. The team featured some of the greatest players in baseball history, including five members now in the Baseball Hall of Fame, such as catcher Josh Gibson. Carnegie Mellon University
64: The Terrible Towel is a gold rally towel that Pittsburgh Steelers fans wave during games. It was created by broadcaster Myron Cope in 1975 for a playoff game and has become one of the most iconic symbols in NFL history. The trademark is owned by the Allegheny Valley School for people with intellectual disabilities, which receives royalties from its sale.
65: The Pittsburgh Steelers have won six Super Bowls — the most in NFL history as of the early 2020s — in 1975, 1976, 1979, 1980, 2006, and 2009.
66: The Pittsburgh Penguins have won five Stanley Cup championships: in 1991 and 1992 (led by Mario Lemieux), in 2009 (led by Sidney Crosby and Evgeni Malkin), and back-to-back in 2016 and 2017.
67: Frick Park is Pittsburgh's largest municipal park, located in the eastern neighborhoods. It is known for its extensive network of hiking and biking trails through natural forest, ravines, and meadows, and is a beloved green space in an otherwise densely urban area.
68: Highland Park is known for the Pittsburgh Zoo & Aquarium, which is located within the park, as well as a large reservoir, walking trails, and an elegant Victorian-era residential neighborhood surrounding it.
69: Pittsburgh's light rail system is operated by Pittsburgh Regional Transit (formerly Port Authority of Allegheny County) and is called the "T." It operates the Red Line (formerly the South Hills Junction lines) connecting downtown Pittsburgh to the South Hills suburbs, and the Blue Line serving the North Shore stadiums.
70: The primary airport serving Pittsburgh is Pittsburgh International Airport (PIT), located in Moon Township about 16 miles west of downtown. Arnold Palmer Regional Airport (LBE) in Latrobe serves as a regional alternative.
71: The Fort Pitt Tunnel is a highway tunnel through Mount Washington carrying Interstate 376, and is one of Pittsburgh's most iconic commuter experiences. Drivers emerge from the tunnel directly into the dramatic panoramic view of Pittsburgh's downtown skyline and the three rivers — a view frequently cited as one of the most stunning urban entrances in America.
72: The major interstates passing through Pittsburgh are I-376 (connecting downtown to the airport), I-79 (running north-south), I-279 (the Veterans Bridge corridor), I-376 (Monroeville to the airport), and the Pennsylvania Turnpike (I-76) which passes through the outer Pittsburgh suburbs.
73: The Tree of Life Synagogue in the Squirrel Hill neighborhood was the site of the deadliest antisemitic attack in American history, on October 27, 2018, when a gunman killed 11 Jewish worshippers during Shabbat morning services. It has become a national symbol of the consequences of hate and antisemitism, and Pittsburgh's community response became a model of resilience and solidarity.
74: Mister Rogers' Neighborhood was produced in Pittsburgh at WQED Studios. Fred Rogers served as creator, host, head writer, lead musical composer, and chief puppeteer and voice talent in the Neighborhood of Make-Believe, running the show for more than 30 years on PBS beginning in 1968. Fred Rogers Productions - Several pieces of art are dedicated to Rogers throughout Pittsburgh, including an eleven-foot, 7,000-pound bronze statue of him in the North Shore neighborhood. Wikipedia
75: Pittsburgh has been a popular filming location for many major productions, including The Dark Knight Rises (2012), Jack Reacher (2012), Mindhunter (Netflix series), The Perks of Being a Wallflower (2012), Silence of the Lambs (1991), Flashdance (1983), Promising Young Woman (2020), and the Avengers films.
76: The Carrie Blast Furnaces are a preserved industrial landmark in Rankin, Pennsylvania (just outside Pittsburgh), part of the former Homestead Steel Works. Built in 1884, they operated until 1978 and are now a National Historic Landmark managed by the Rivers of Steel Heritage Area, offering tours and serving as a testament to Pittsburgh's steel-making legacy.
